input.content:
# Plan: Expand Command Palette with New Commands

## Context

The command palette currently has only 5 commands (global search, playlist search, global favorites, downloads, global recent). The app has many more navigable destinations and actions that would benefit from quick keyboard access — settings, sources/playlist management, adding playlists, refreshing, playlist info, account info, and section-level navigation within portals.

## New Commands to Add

### Global Scope (always available)

| ID | Label | Description | Icon | Enabled When |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `open-settings` | Open settings | Navigate to application settings | `settings` | Always |
| `open-sources` | Manage sources | Navigate to source management | `library_books` | Always |
| `add-playlist` | Add new source | Open add playlist dialog | `add_circle_outline` | Always |
| `open-dashboard` | Open dashboard | Navigate to the dashboard | `dashboard` | Always |

### Playlist Scope (when a playlist is active)

| ID | Label | Description | Icon | Enabled When |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `refresh-playlist` | Refresh playlist | Re-sync data from remote source | `sync` | Xtream or Stalker playlist active |
| `playlist-info` | Playlist details | Show current playlist information | `info` | Any playlist active |
| `account-info` | Account info | Show Xtream server account details | `account_circle` | Xtream playlist active |

### Section Scope (contextual navigation within active portal)

| ID | Label | Description | Icon | Enabled When |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `go-to-vod` | Movies | Jump to movies section | `movie` | Xtream or Stalker active |
| `go-to-live` | Live TV | Jump to live TV section | `live_tv` | Xtream or Stalker active |
| `go-to-series` | Series | Jump to series section | `video_library` | Xtream or Stalker active |

## Files to Modify

### 1. `workspace-command-palette.component.ts` (type only)
**Path**: `libs/workspace/shell/feature/src/lib/workspace-command-palette/workspace-command-palette.component.ts`

Expand the `WorkspaceCommandId` union type with the new IDs:
```typescript
export type WorkspaceCommandId =
    | 'global-search'
    | 'playlist-search'
    | 'open-global-favorites'
    | 'open-downloads'
    | 'open-global-recent'
    | 'open-settings'
    | 'open-sources'
    | 'open-dashboard'
    | 'add-playlist'
    | 'refresh-playlist'
    | 'playlist-info'
    | 'account-info'
    | 'go-to-vod'
    | 'go-to-live'
    | 'go-to-series';
```

Update `getCommandIcon()` with icons for all new commands.

### 2. `workspace-shell.facade.ts` (definitions + handler)
**Path**: `libs/workspace/shell/feature/src/lib/workspace-shell/services/workspace-shell.facade.ts`

**a) Add new entries to `WORKSPACE_COMMAND_DEFINITIONS`** array, after existing entries. Each definition uses the same `{ id, labelKey, descriptionKey, scope, isEnabled }` shape.

Availability state needs new fields in `WorkspaceCommandAvailability`:
```typescript
interface WorkspaceCommandAvailability {
    context: WorkspacePortalContext | null;
    dashboardXtreamContext: WorkspacePortalContext | null;
    hasXtreamPlaylists: boolean;
    isElectron: boolean;
    canRefreshPlaylist: boolean;    // NEW
    hasActivePlaylist: boolean;     // NEW
    isXtreamPlaylist: boolean;      // NEW
    isPortalPlaylist: boolean;      // NEW — true for xtreams or stalker
}
```

Update `getCommandPaletteItems()` to populate these new fields from existing computed signals (`canRefreshPlaylist()`, `activePlaylist()`, `currentContext()`).

**b) Expand `runCommandPaletteAction()`** switch with handlers for each new command:
- `open-settings` → `this.router.navigate(['/workspace', 'settings'])`
- `open-sources` → `this.router.navigate(['/workspace', 'sources'])`
- `open-dashboard` → `this.router.navigate(['/workspace', 'dashboard'])`
- `add-playlist` → `this.openAddPlaylistDialog()`
- `refresh-playlist` → `this.refreshCurrentPlaylist()`
- `playlist-info` → `this.openPlaylistInfo()`
- `account-info` → `this.openAccountInfo()`
- `go-to-vod` → navigate to `vod` section of current portal
- `go-to-live` → navigate to `live`/`itv` section of current portal
- `go-to-series` → navigate to `series` section of current portal

Section navigation will use the current context's provider and playlistId:
```typescript
const ctx = this.currentContext();
this.router.navigate(['/workspace', ctx.provider, ctx.playlistId, section]);
```

### 3. Translation files (en.json, de.json, ru.json)
Add under `WORKSPACE.SHELL.COMMANDS`:

**en.json:**
```json
"OPEN_SETTINGS_LABEL": "Open settings",
"OPEN_SETTINGS_DESCRIPTION": "Navigate to application settings",
"OPEN_SOURCES_LABEL": "Manage sources",
"OPEN_SOURCES_DESCRIPTION": "Navigate to source management",
"OPEN_DASHBOARD_LABEL": "Open dashboard",
"OPEN_DASHBOARD_DESCRIPTION": "Navigate to the dashboard",
"ADD_PLAYLIST_LABEL": "Add new source",
"ADD_PLAYLIST_DESCRIPTION": "Open add playlist dialog",
"REFRESH_PLAYLIST_LABEL": "Refresh playlist",
"REFRESH_PLAYLIST_DESCRIPTION": "Re-sync data from remote source",
"PLAYLIST_INFO_LABEL": "Playlist details",
"PLAYLIST_INFO_DESCRIPTION": "Show current playlist information",
"ACCOUNT_INFO_LABEL": "Account info",
"ACCOUNT_INFO_DESCRIPTION": "Show Xtream server account details",
"GO_TO_VOD_LABEL": "Movies",
"GO_TO_VOD_DESCRIPTION": "Jump to movies section",
"GO_TO_LIVE_LABEL": "Live TV",
"GO_TO_LIVE_DESCRIPTION": "Jump to live TV section",
"GO_TO_SERIES_LABEL": "Series",
"GO_TO_SERIES_DESCRIPTION": "Jump to series section"
```

**de.json** and **ru.json**: Equivalent translations.

## Verification

1. `nx serve web` — open command palette with Ctrl/Cmd+K
2. On dashboard (no playlist): see global commands (settings, sources, dashboard, add, favorites, recent, downloads)
3. Open an Xtream playlist: see playlist commands (refresh, info, account info) and section commands (movies, live, series)
4. Open a Stalker playlist: see playlist commands (refresh, info — no account info) and section commands
5. Click each command — verify it performs the correct action
6. Disabled commands (e.g. refresh when not on portal) should show as disabled
7. `nx lint workspace-shell-feature` passes
8. `nx build web --configuration=pwa` compiles

lines: -# Plan: Command Palette Redesign — Spotlight/Raycast Style
+# Plan: Expand Command Palette with New Commands

-The current command palette (`workspace-command-palette`) has a nested box-in-box layout: a `.palette-shell` container wrapping a `.palette-search-panel` wrapper around the search input. This creates unnecessary visual weight — double borders, double backgrounds, and extra padding. The user wants a cleaner, Spotlight/Raycast-inspired design that uses the same compact inline search style already established in the context-panel and category-management-dialog.

+The command palette currently has only 5 commands (global search, playlist search, global favorites, downloads, global recent). The app has many more navigable destinations and actions that would benefit from quick keyboard access — settings, sources/playlist management, adding playlists, refreshing, playlist info, account info, and section-level navigation within portals.

-**Aesthetic**: Clean, utilitarian — inspired by macOS Spotlight and Raycast. Single-surface floating panel, no nested containers, search input as the hero element at the top. Tight spacing, crisp separators, subtle hover states. The palette should feel like a fast launcher, not a dialog.
-- **Flat hierarchy**: Remove the `.palette-search-panel` wrapper — the search input sits directly in the palette shell with the same `search-inline` styling
-- **Remove the header** (title + subtitle) — Spotlight/Raycast don't announce themselves. The search input IS the identity
-- **Tighter command rows**: More compact, single-line items with icon + label + description inline (not stacked). Closer to Raycast's row density
-- **Keyboard hint in search**: Keep the `Ctrl/Cmd+K` badge inside the search row
-- **Scope labels**: Keep group headings but make them more subtle — tiny uppercase labels like Raycast's section headers
-- **Selected state**: Use the existing `--app-selection-*` variables for consistency with nav-list items
+| ID | Label | Description | Icon | Enabled When |
+| `open-settings` | Open settings | Navigate to application settings | `settings` | Always |
+| `open-sources` | Manage sources | Navigate to source management | `library_books` | Always |
+| `add-playlist` | Add new source | Open add playlist dialog | `add_circle_outline` | Always |
+| `open-dashboard` | Open dashboard | Navigate to the dashboard | `dashboard` | Always |
+### Playlist Scope (when a playlist is active)
+| ID | Label | Description | Icon | Enabled When |
+| `refresh-playlist` | Refresh playlist | Re-sync data from remote source | `sync` | Xtream or Stalker playlist active |
+| `playlist-info` | Playlist details | Show current playlist information | `info` | Any playlist active |
+| `account-info` | Account info | Show Xtream server account details | `account_circle` | Xtream playlist active |
+### Section Scope (contextual navigation within active portal)
+| ID | Label | Description | Icon | Enabled When |
+| `go-to-vod` | Movies | Jump to movies section | `movie` | Xtream or Stalker active |
+| `go-to-live` | Live TV | Jump to live TV section | `live_tv` | Xtream or Stalker active |
+| `go-to-series` | Series | Jump to series section | `video_library` | Xtream or Stalker active |
-Change command item layout: put icon, label, and description in a single horizontal row instead of stacking label/description vertically:
+Expand the `WorkspaceCommandId` union type with the new IDs:
+Update `getCommandIcon()` with icons for all new commands.
-- **`.palette-shell`**: Keep the floating panel (`border-radius: 14px`, shadow, border). Remove inner padding (content handles its own).

-- **`.palette-search`**: Same inline search style — flex row, `gap: 8px`, `min-height: 40px`, `padding: 0 14px`. No border/background — it inherits from the shell. The search IS the top of the palette. Slightly larger input font (~0.94rem) since it's the hero element.
-- **`.palette-divider`**: Thin `1px` separator between search and results using `--mat-sys-outline-variant`.
-- **`.palette-command`**: Horizontal flex row (`display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 10px`), `padding: 6px 8px`, `border-radius: 8px`. Single line layout.
-- **`.palette-command__description`**: `font-size: 0.76rem`, `color: on-surface-variant`, `margin-left: auto` to push right (Raycast style).
-- **Selected/hover**: Use `--app-selection-surface` gradient + border, matching the nav-list `.selected` pattern.
+**a) Add new entries to `WORKSPACE_COMMAND_DEFINITIONS`** array, after existing entries. Each definition uses the same `{ id, labelKey, descriptionKey, scope, isEnabled }` shape.
+    isPortalPlaylist: boolean;      // NEW — true for xtreams or stalker
-No functional changes needed — only the template structure changes. The `getCommandIcon` method now returns icons for all scopes (not just global) so every command gets an icon:
+Update `getCommandPaletteItems()` to populate these new fields from existing computed signals (`canRefreshPlaylist()`, `activePlaylist()`, `currentContext()`).
+**b) Expand `runCommandPaletteAction()`** switch with handlers for each new command:
+- `go-to-vod` → navigate to `vod` section of current portal
+- `go-to-live` → navigate to `live`/`itv` section of current portal
+- `go-to-series` → navigate to `series` section of current portal
-Update `getCommandIcon` to always return an icon string (never null).
+Section navigation will use the current context's provider and playlistId:
-- Dialog open config in `workspace-shell.facade.ts` — width/panelClass stays the same
-- The transparent dialog surface override stays
-1. `nx serve web` — open the command palette with Ctrl/Cmd+K
-2. Visual: single-surface panel, no nested box, search input at top with no extra wrapper
-3. Commands display as horizontal rows: icon | label | description (right-aligned)
-4. Arrow key navigation highlights rows correctly
-6. Empty state shows when no results match
+1. `nx serve web` — open command palette with Ctrl/Cmd+K
+2. On dashboard (no playlist): see global commands (settings, sources, dashboard, add, favorites, recent, downloads)
+3. Open an Xtream playlist: see playlist commands (refresh, info, account info) and section commands (movies, live, series)
+4. Open a Stalker playlist: see playlist commands (refresh, info — no account info) and section commands
+5. Click each command — verify it performs the correct action
+6. Disabled commands (e.g. refresh when not on portal) should show as disabled

# Plan: Command Palette Redesign — Spotlight/Raycast Style ## Context The current command palette (`workspace-command-palette`) has a nested box-in-box layout: a `.palette-shell` container wrapping a `.palette-search-panel` wrapper around the search input. This creates unnecessary visual weight — double borders, double backgrounds, and extra padding. The user wants a cleaner, Spotlight/Raycast-inspired design that uses the same compact inline search style already established in the context-panel and category-management-dialog. ## Design Direction **Aesthetic**: Clean, utilitarian — inspired by macOS Spotlight and Raycast. Single-surface floating panel, no nested containers, search input as the hero element at the top. Tight spacing, crisp separators, subtle hover states. The palette should feel like a fast launcher, not a dialog. Key principles: - **Flat hierarchy**: Remove the `.palette-search-panel` wrapper — the search input sits directly in the palette shell with the same `search-inline` styling - **Remove the header** (title + subtitle) — Spotlight/Raycast don't announce themselves. The search input IS the identity - **Tighter command rows**: More compact, single-line items with icon + label + description inline (not stacked). Closer to Raycast's row density - **Keyboard hint in search**: Keep the `Ctrl/Cmd+K` badge inside the search row - **Scope labels**: Keep group headings but make them more subtle — tiny uppercase labels like Raycast's section headers - **Selected state**: Use the existing `--app-selection-*` variables for consistency with nav-list items ## Files to Modify ### 1. `workspace-command-palette.component.html` **Path**: `libs/workspace/shell/feature/src/lib/workspace-command-palette/workspace-command-palette.component.html` Remove the `
` and `.palette-search-panel` wrapper. Restructure to: ```html
``` Change command item layout: put icon, label, and description in a single horizontal row instead of stacking label/description vertically: ```html ``` ### 2. `workspace-command-palette.component.scss` **Path**: `libs/workspace/shell/feature/src/lib/workspace-command-palette/workspace-command-palette.component.scss` Full restyle: - **`.palette-shell`**: Keep the floating panel (`border-radius: 14px`, shadow, border). Remove inner padding (content handles its own). - **Remove** `.palette-header`, `.palette-search-panel` styles entirely. - **`.palette-search`**: Same inline search style — flex row, `gap: 8px`, `min-height: 40px`, `padding: 0 14px`. No border/background — it inherits from the shell. The search IS the top of the palette. Slightly larger input font (~0.94rem) since it's the hero element. - **`.palette-divider`**: Thin `1px` separator between search and results using `--mat-sys-outline-variant`. - **`.palette-results`**: Padding `6px 6px 6px`, reduced gap. - **`.palette-group h3`**: Tiny uppercase label, `padding: 6px 8px 2px`, `font-size: 0.68rem`. - **`.palette-command`**: Horizontal flex row (`display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 10px`), `padding: 6px 8px`, `border-radius: 8px`. Single line layout. - **`.palette-command__icon`**: 28px square, `border-radius: 7px`, subtle background. - **`.palette-command__label`**: `font-size: 0.84rem`, `font-weight: 540`, truncate. - **`.palette-command__description`**: `font-size: 0.76rem`, `color: on-surface-variant`, `margin-left: auto` to push right (Raycast style). - **Selected/hover**: Use `--app-selection-surface` gradient + border, matching the nav-list `.selected` pattern. - **`.palette-empty`**: Compact centered message. ### 3. `workspace-command-palette.component.ts` **Path**: `libs/workspace/shell/feature/src/lib/workspace-command-palette/workspace-command-palette.component.ts` No functional changes needed — only the template structure changes. The `getCommandIcon` method now returns icons for all scopes (not just global) so every command gets an icon: - `playlist-search` → `'playlist_play'` - Keep existing global icons - Default fallback → `'bolt'` Update `getCommandIcon` to always return an icon string (never null). ## What NOT to Change - Dialog open config in `workspace-shell.facade.ts` — width/panelClass stays the same - The transparent dialog surface override stays - Keyboard navigation logic stays identical - Command grouping/filtering logic stays identical ## Verification 1. `nx serve web` — open the command palette with Ctrl/Cmd+K 2. Visual: single-surface panel, no nested box, search input at top with no extra wrapper 3. Commands display as horizontal rows: icon | label | description (right-aligned) 4. Arrow key navigation highlights rows correctly 5. Enter selects, Escape closes 6. Empty state shows when no results match 7. `nx lint workspace-shell-feature` passes 8. `nx build web --configuration=pwa` compiles

input.plan:
# Plan: Expand Command Palette with New Commands

## Context

The command palette currently has only 5 commands (global search, playlist search, global favorites, downloads, global recent). The app has many more navigable destinations and actions that would benefit from quick keyboard access — settings, sources/playlist management, adding playlists, refreshing, playlist info, account info, and section-level navigation within portals.

## New Commands to Add

### Global Scope (always available)

| ID | Label | Description | Icon | Enabled When |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `open-settings` | Open settings | Navigate to application settings | `settings` | Always |
| `open-sources` | Manage sources | Navigate to source management | `library_books` | Always |
| `add-playlist` | Add new source | Open add playlist dialog | `add_circle_outline` | Always |
| `open-dashboard` | Open dashboard | Navigate to the dashboard | `dashboard` | Always |

### Playlist Scope (when a playlist is active)

| ID | Label | Description | Icon | Enabled When |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `refresh-playlist` | Refresh playlist | Re-sync data from remote source | `sync` | Xtream or Stalker playlist active |
| `playlist-info` | Playlist details | Show current playlist information | `info` | Any playlist active |
| `account-info` | Account info | Show Xtream server account details | `account_circle` | Xtream playlist active |

### Section Scope (contextual navigation within active portal)

| ID | Label | Description | Icon | Enabled When |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `go-to-vod` | Movies | Jump to movies section | `movie` | Xtream or Stalker active |
| `go-to-live` | Live TV | Jump to live TV section | `live_tv` | Xtream or Stalker active |
| `go-to-series` | Series | Jump to series section | `video_library` | Xtream or Stalker active |

## Files to Modify

### 1. `workspace-command-palette.component.ts` (type only)
**Path**: `libs/workspace/shell/feature/src/lib/workspace-command-palette/workspace-command-palette.component.ts`

Expand the `WorkspaceCommandId` union type with the new IDs:
```typescript
export type WorkspaceCommandId =
    | 'global-search'
    | 'playlist-search'
    | 'open-global-favorites'
    | 'open-downloads'
    | 'open-global-recent'
    | 'open-settings'
    | 'open-sources'
    | 'open-dashboard'
    | 'add-playlist'
    | 'refresh-playlist'
    | 'playlist-info'
    | 'account-info'
    | 'go-to-vod'
    | 'go-to-live'
    | 'go-to-series';
```

Update `getCommandIcon()` with icons for all new commands.

### 2. `workspace-shell.facade.ts` (definitions + handler)
**Path**: `libs/workspace/shell/feature/src/lib/workspace-shell/services/workspace-shell.facade.ts`

**a) Add new entries to `WORKSPACE_COMMAND_DEFINITIONS`** array, after existing entries. Each definition uses the same `{ id, labelKey, descriptionKey, scope, isEnabled }` shape.

Availability state needs new fields in `WorkspaceCommandAvailability`:
```typescript
interface WorkspaceCommandAvailability {
    context: WorkspacePortalContext | null;
    dashboardXtreamContext: WorkspacePortalContext | null;
    hasXtreamPlaylists: boolean;
    isElectron: boolean;
    canRefreshPlaylist: boolean;    // NEW
    hasActivePlaylist: boolean;     // NEW
    isXtreamPlaylist: boolean;      // NEW
    isPortalPlaylist: boolean;      // NEW — true for xtreams or stalker
}
```

Update `getCommandPaletteItems()` to populate these new fields from existing computed signals (`canRefreshPlaylist()`, `activePlaylist()`, `currentContext()`).

**b) Expand `runCommandPaletteAction()`** switch with handlers for each new command:
- `open-settings` → `this.router.navigate(['/workspace', 'settings'])`
- `open-sources` → `this.router.navigate(['/workspace', 'sources'])`
- `open-dashboard` → `this.router.navigate(['/workspace', 'dashboard'])`
- `add-playlist` → `this.openAddPlaylistDialog()`
- `refresh-playlist` → `this.refreshCurrentPlaylist()`
- `playlist-info` → `this.openPlaylistInfo()`
- `account-info` → `this.openAccountInfo()`
- `go-to-vod` → navigate to `vod` section of current portal
- `go-to-live` → navigate to `live`/`itv` section of current portal
- `go-to-series` → navigate to `series` section of current portal

Section navigation will use the current context's provider and playlistId:
```typescript
const ctx = this.currentContext();
this.router.navigate(['/workspace', ctx.provider, ctx.playlistId, section]);
```

### 3. Translation files (en.json, de.json, ru.json)
Add under `WORKSPACE.SHELL.COMMANDS`:

**en.json:**
```json
"OPEN_SETTINGS_LABEL": "Open settings",
"OPEN_SETTINGS_DESCRIPTION": "Navigate to application settings",
"OPEN_SOURCES_LABEL": "Manage sources",
"OPEN_SOURCES_DESCRIPTION": "Navigate to source management",
"OPEN_DASHBOARD_LABEL": "Open dashboard",
"OPEN_DASHBOARD_DESCRIPTION": "Navigate to the dashboard",
"ADD_PLAYLIST_LABEL": "Add new source",
"ADD_PLAYLIST_DESCRIPTION": "Open add playlist dialog",
"REFRESH_PLAYLIST_LABEL": "Refresh playlist",
"REFRESH_PLAYLIST_DESCRIPTION": "Re-sync data from remote source",
"PLAYLIST_INFO_LABEL": "Playlist details",
"PLAYLIST_INFO_DESCRIPTION": "Show current playlist information",
"ACCOUNT_INFO_LABEL": "Account info",
"ACCOUNT_INFO_DESCRIPTION": "Show Xtream server account details",
"GO_TO_VOD_LABEL": "Movies",
"GO_TO_VOD_DESCRIPTION": "Jump to movies section",
"GO_TO_LIVE_LABEL": "Live TV",
"GO_TO_LIVE_DESCRIPTION": "Jump to live TV section",
"GO_TO_SERIES_LABEL": "Series",
"GO_TO_SERIES_DESCRIPTION": "Jump to series section"
```

**de.json** and **ru.json**: Equivalent translations.

## Verification

1. `nx serve web` — open command palette with Ctrl/Cmd+K
2. On dashboard (no playlist): see global commands (settings, sources, dashboard, add, favorites, recent, downloads)
3. Open an Xtream playlist: see playlist commands (refresh, info, account info) and section commands (movies, live, series)
4. Open a Stalker playlist: see playlist commands (refresh, info — no account info) and section commands
5. Click each command — verify it performs the correct action
6. Disabled commands (e.g. refresh when not on portal) should show as disabled
7. `nx lint workspace-shell-feature` passes
8. `nx build web --configuration=pwa` compiles

content.text:
Clean lint and build. Here's a summary of what was added:

**10 new commands** across 3 scopes:

**Global** (always available):
- **Open settings** (`settings`) — navigates to `/workspace/settings`
- **Manage sources** (`library_books`) — navigates to `/workspace/sources`
- **Open dashboard** (`dashboard`) — navigates to `/workspace/dashboard`
- **Add new source** (`add_circle_outline`) — opens the add playlist dialog

**Playlist** (contextual):
- **Refresh playlist** (`sync`) — re-syncs from remote source (Xtream/Stalker only)
- **Playlist details** (`info`) — shows current playlist info (any active playlist)
- **Account info** (`account_circle`) — shows Xtream server account details (Xtream only)

**Section** (portal navigation):
- **Movies** (`movie`) — jumps to vod section
- **Live TV** (`live_tv`) — jumps to live/itv section (adapts to Stalker's `itv` route)
- **Series** (`video_library`) — jumps to series section

The `WorkspaceCommandAvailability` interface was extended with `canRefreshPlaylist`, `hasActivePlaylist`, `isXtreamPlaylist`, and `isPortalPlaylist` fields to control enablement. All 3 translation files (en/de/ru) updated.

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/4gray/.claude/plugins/marketplaces/anthropic-agent-skills/skills/frontend-design

This skill guides creation of distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces that avoid generic "AI slop" aesthetics. Implement real working code with exceptional attention to aesthetic details and creative choices.

The user provides frontend requirements: a component, page, application, or interface to build. They may include context about the purpose, audience, or technical constraints.

## Design Thinking

Before coding, understand the context and commit to a BOLD aesthetic direction:
- **Purpose**: What problem does this interface solve? Who uses it?
- **Tone**: Pick an extreme: brutally minimal, maximalist chaos, retro-futuristic, organic/natural, luxury/refined, playful/toy-like, editorial/magazine, brutalist/raw, art deco/geometric, soft/pastel, industrial/utilitarian, etc. There are so many flavors to choose from. Use these for inspiration but design one that is true to the aesthetic direction.
- **Constraints**: Technical requirements (framework, performance, accessibility).
- **Differentiation**: What makes this UNFORGETTABLE? What's the one thing someone will remember?

**CRITICAL**: Choose a clear conceptual direction and execute it with precision. Bold maximalism and refined minimalism both work - the key is intentionality, not intensity.

Then implement working code (HTML/CSS/JS, React, Vue, etc.) that is:
- Production-grade and functional
- Visually striking and memorable
- Cohesive with a clear aesthetic point-of-view
- Meticulously refined in every detail

## Frontend Aesthetics Guidelines

Focus on:
- **Typography**: Choose fonts that are beautiful, unique, and interesting. Avoid generic fonts like Arial and Inter; opt instead for distinctive choices that elevate the frontend's aesthetics; unexpected, characterful font choices. Pair a distinctive display font with a refined body font.
- **Color & Theme**: Commit to a cohesive aesthetic. Use CSS variables for consistency. Dominant colors with sharp accents outperform timid, evenly-distributed palettes.
- **Motion**: Use animations for effects and micro-interactions. Prioritize CSS-only solutions for HTML. Use Motion library for React when available. Focus on high-impact moments: one well-orchestrated page load with staggered reveals (animation-delay) creates more delight than scattered micro-interactions. Use scroll-triggering and hover states that surprise.
- **Spatial Composition**: Unexpected layouts. Asymmetry. Overlap. Diagonal flow. Grid-breaking elements. Generous negative space OR controlled density.
- **Backgrounds & Visual Details**: Create atmosphere and depth rather than defaulting to solid colors. Add contextual effects and textures that match the overall aesthetic. Apply creative forms like gradient meshes, noise textures, geometric patterns, layered transparencies, dramatic shadows, decorative borders, custom cursors, and grain overlays.

NEVER use generic AI-generated aesthetics like overused font families (Inter, Roboto, Arial, system fonts), cliched color schemes (particularly purple gradients on white backgrounds), predictable layouts and component patterns, and cookie-cutter design that lacks context-specific character.

Interpret creatively and make unexpected choices that feel genuinely designed for the context. No design should be the same. Vary between light and dark themes, different fonts, different aesthetics. NEVER converge on common choices (Space Grotesk, for example) across generations.

**IMPORTANT**: Match implementation complexity to the aesthetic vision. Maximalist designs need elaborate code with extensive animations and effects. Minimalist or refined designs need restraint, precision, and careful attention to spacing, typography, and subtle details. Elegance comes from executing the vision well.

Remember: Claude is capable of extraordinary creative work. Don't hold back, show what can truly be created when thinking outside the box and committing fully to a distinctive vision.

content.text:
Done. Here's what was added:

**Visual effect**: When downloads are active, the download icon in the header gets two layered animations:

1. **Spinning progress ring** — An SVG with two circles overlaid on the button: a faint track circle (`15% primary`) and an arc segment (`stroke-dasharray: 30 64`) that continuously rotates (`1.4s linear infinite`). This creates a subtle orbital spinner around the download icon, similar to how macOS shows activity on dock icons.

2. **Icon pulse** — The download icon itself gently pulses opacity (`1 → 0.55 → 1` over `2s ease-in-out infinite`), giving a breathing effect that signals ongoing activity without being distracting.

Both animations only appear when `hasActiveDownloads` is true (queued or downloading items exist). The SVG ring is conditionally rendered with `@if` so it's not in the DOM when idle.

**Data flow**: `DownloadsService.activeCount()` (already existed, `providedIn: 'root'`) → `WorkspaceShellFacade.hasActiveDownloads` (new computed) → `WorkspaceShellHeaderComponent.hasActiveDownloads` (new input) → template conditional + CSS classes.

content.text:
The SVG is inside a `mat-icon-button`. Material buttons use `overflow: hidden` on the button and its internal content wrappers, which clips the absolutely positioned SVG. The SVG also sits inside the button's content projection area which has its own stacking.

The fix: move the SVG outside the button's content flow using a wrapper `div` with `position: relative`, or better — use a `::before`/`::after` pseudo-element approach instead of inline SVG.

Let me check what Material does to the button's internals:

The issue is that `mat-icon-button` wraps content in a `.mdc-button__label` span with `overflow: hidden`, plus the button itself has `overflow: visible` overridden by Material's ripple layer. The SVG inside the button content gets clipped.

Best fix: wrap the button in a `<span>` with `position: relative` and put the SVG as a sibling, outside the button entirely.

content.text:
Fixed. The SVG ring was being clipped because it was inside the `mat-icon-button`'s content area, which Material wraps in an internal `.mdc-button__label` with `overflow: hidden`.

The fix moves the SVG **outside** the button as a sibling, both wrapped in a `<span class="download-btn-wrap">` with `position: relative; display: inline-flex`. The ring now positions itself absolutely over the wrapper (which matches the button's dimensions), so it renders on top without being clipped by Material's internals.

content.text: Let me inspect the actual rendered DOM to understand the Material button structure:

The `mat-icon-button` has `overflow: hidden` set by Material at the host level, and Angular's view encapsulation means our SVG styles might not pierce through. Let me take a completely different approach — use a pure CSS `::after` pseudo-element on the wrapper span instead of an inline SVG. This avoids all Material DOM conflicts.

## angular-cli

<General Purpose>
This server provides a safe, programmatic interface to the Angular CLI for an AI assistant.
Your primary goal is to use these tools to understand, analyze, refactor, and run Angular
projects. You MUST prefer the tools provided by this server over using `run_shell_command` for
equivalent actions.
</General Purpose>

<Core Workflows & Tool Guide>
* **1. Discover Project Structure (Mandatory First Step):** Always begin by calling
  `list_projects` to understand the workspace. The `path` property for a workspace
  is a required input for other tools.

* **2. Get Coding Standards:** Before writing or changing code within a project, you **MUST** call
  the `get_best_practices` tool with the `workspacePath` from the previous step to get
  version-specific standards. For general knowledge, you can call the tool without this path.

* **3. Answer User Questions:**
    - For conceptual questions ("what is..."), use `search_documentation`.
    - For code examples ("show me how to..."), use `find_examples`.
</Core Workflows & Tool Guide>

<Key Concepts>
* **Workspace vs. Project:** A 'workspace' contains an `angular.json` file and defines 'projects'
  (applications or libraries). A monorepo can have multiple workspaces.
* **Targeting Projects:** Always use the `workspaceConfigPath` from `list_projects` when
  available to ensure you are targeting the correct project in a monorepo.
</Key Concepts>

## claude-in-chrome
**IMPORTANT: Before using any chrome browser tools, you MUST first load them using ToolSearch.**

Chrome browser tools are MCP tools that require loading before use. Before calling any mcp__claude-in-chrome__* tool:
1. Use ToolSearch with `select:mcp__claude-in-chrome__<tool_name>` to load the specific tool
2. Then call the tool

For example, to get tab context:
1. First: ToolSearch with query "select:mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_context_mcp"
2. Then: Call mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_context_mcp

## context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer -- your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

## nx-mcp

The Nx MCP server provides tools for working with Nx monorepo workspaces. Use it to:

  - Understand workspace architecture, project dependencies, and configuration
  - Retrieve up-to-date Nx documentation for configuration questions and best practices
  - Explore individual project details, targets, and how to run them
  - Discover available generators and scaffold new code
  - Visualize project and task dependency graphs
  - Monitor running tasks and view their output
  - Analyze CI/CD pipeline performance and history from Nx Cloud
  - Interact with Nx Cloud self-healing CI

  Any kind of work inside the Nx workspace or monorepo as well when working with CI can make use of the Nx MCP server.

content.text:
Found it! **`borderTopWidth: "0px"` and `borderTopStyle: "none"`** — the border is not being applied at all. Angular's view encapsulation is scoping the `border` shorthand property and it's being overridden or not applied. The `color-mix` in the border value might be the issue, or Material's button styles are resetting borders on all elements within.

Let me check what's overriding it:

input.description: Find CSS rules resetting border on the ring element
Force inline border style and check if it renders

content.content: "{\"borderTopWidth\":\"2px\",\"borderTopStyle\":\"solid\",\"borderTopColor\":\"rgb(255, 0, 0)\",\"width\":\"44px\",\"height\":\"44px\",\"boxSizing\":\"content-box\"}"
[32m✓[0m Screenshot saved to [32m/tmp/ring-test.png[0m

content.text:
I can see the red ring in the top-right corner around the download icon! So the inline style works, but the CSS `border` property from the stylesheet is being stripped. The issue is the `color-mix()` function in the border value — the Angular CSS compiler might not support it, or the shorthand is being parsed incorrectly.

Let me fix the SCSS to use individual border properties with simpler color values:

content.content: The file /Users/4gray/Code/iptvnator/libs/workspace/shell/feature/src/lib/workspace-shell/components/workspace-shell-header/workspace-shell-header.component.scss has been updated successfully.

content.text: Actually, `rgba(var(...))` might also be problematic. Let me use the simplest possible approach:

content.content: The file /Users/4gray/Code/iptvnator/libs/workspace/shell/feature/src/lib/workspace-shell/components/workspace-shell-header/workspace-shell-header.component.scss has been updated successfully.

content.content: "{\"borderTopWidth\":\"2px\",\"borderTopStyle\":\"solid\",\"borderTopColor\":\"rgb(230, 225, 230)\",\"borderRightColor\":\"rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.1)\",\"borderRightWidth\":\"2px\"}"

content.text: The border is now being applied — `borderTopWidth: "2px"` and `borderRightColor: "rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.1)"`. Let me take a screenshot to confirm it's visible.

input.description: Screenshot to verify ring is visible

content.content: [32m✓[0m Screenshot saved to [32m/tmp/ring-fixed.png[0m

content.text:
I can see a spinning ring around the download icon in the top-right! The fix worked — the root cause was that `color-mix()` in the `border` shorthand was being stripped by Angular's CSS compiler, resulting in `border-width: 0px`. Using explicit `border-width`, `border-style`, and `border-color` with plain `rgba()` resolves it.

The download icon now shows:
- A spinning ring around it (primary color top, subtle white track)
- The pulse animation on the icon itself

`--mat-sys-primary` is empty on this element! Angular's theme variables aren't inherited into this component's DOM. The `border-top-color: var(--mat-sys-primary)` worked on the ring earlier because the computed value showed `rgb(230, 225, 230)` — which is actually the fallback color, not the primary.

Let me check what theme variable is actually available:

"{
  "--mat-sys-primary": "",

  "--app-selection-color": "#78adff",
  "--mat-icon-button-state-layer-color": "#e8e0eb",
  "selectionColor": "#78adff",
  "selectionSurface": "color-mix( in srgb, #78adff 18%, transparent )"

content.text: `--app-selection-color: #78adff` is the available accent color. Let me use that instead:

content.text: Now the fill has the right color. Let me screenshot:

content.text: I can see a tiny blue progress bar sliding beneath the download icon in the top-right header area. It's subtle and elegant — a slim 18px wide, 2px tall animated bar with the accent color (`#78adff`) sliding back and forth over a faint track. Much cleaner than the circle approach.
